flashcards.Back: A standardized protocol used for sending system log or event messages to a specific server. it allows for centralized monitoring of device health and security.
A scale from 0 to 7 used to indicate the importance of a log message. Lower numbers represent more critical events.
The highest syslog severity level, indicating that the system is unusable. This is reserved for catastrophic failures.
A syslog severity level indicating that immediate action is required to correct a serious problem.
A syslog severity level for critical conditions that should be addressed as soon as possible to prevent a service outage.
A syslog severity level used for non-critical error conditions that may impact a specific feature or interface.
A syslog severity level used to notify administrators of potential problems that could become serious if ignored.
A syslog severity level for significant but normal operational events, such as an interface changing state (UP/DOWN).
A syslog severity level for general information messages that do not require any administrative action.
The lowest syslog severity level, containing highly detailed information used only for troubleshooting specific software issues.
A label in a log message that identifies the source system or process that generated the event (e.g., %LINEPROTO or %LINK).
A short, capitalized text string that uniquely identifies the specific type of event within a facility (e.g., UPDOWN).
The part of a log message that records the exact date and time the event occurred, provided the system clock is accurate.
A Cisco command that sends log messages to the physical console port. this is enabled by default at the debugging level.
A command that sends log messages to virtual terminal sessions (Telnet/SSH). users must also run 'terminal monitor' to see them.
A configuration that stores log messages in the device's local RAM. administrators can view these using the 'show logging' command.
The Cisco command used to specify the IP address of an external syslog server where the device should send its logs.
A command that defines the severity threshold for messages sent to an external syslog server. only messages at this level or higher will be forwarded.
The global configuration command used to enable the inclusion of date and time information in all system log messages.
A command that adds a sequential number to every log message, allowing administrators to detect if any logs were missed during transmission.
The primary verification command that displays the status of all logging destinations and the contents of the local log buffer.
The standard destination port used by network devices when forwarding syslog messages to a centralized management server.
The amount of RAM (in bytes) allocated for local log storage. when the buffer is full, the oldest messages are overwritten by new ones.
The standard structure: [timestamp]: %[facility]-[severity]-[mnemonic]: [description].
The process of sending system events over the network to a central repository for archival, correlation, and analysis.
An EXEC mode command required for an SSH/Telnet user to view log messages in their current terminal session.
The process of analyzing log messages from multiple devices to identify patterns or the root cause of a widespread network issue.
The modern IETF standard that defines the syslog protocol, replacing the older RFC 3164 and providing a more extensible format.
The practice of collecting logs from various network sources into a single platform like Splunk or ELK for unified management.
A line configuration command that prevents log messages from interrupting command-line input by moving them to a new line.
